entry.title: Setting Up SAP Joule on S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: A Technical Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP’s generative AI copilot designed to enhance user productivity across SAP applications. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, Joule empowers users to discover applications, access contextual business insights, and navigate business processes conversationally directly within the SAP Fiori experience.

This document provides a comprehensive, end-to-end technical guide for setting up SAP Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition. It is written from an administrator and platform engineering perspective, explaining not only how to configure each component, but also why it is required and how the overall integration functions.

Architecture Overview

 

At a high level, Joule runs on SAP BTP and is embedded into the SAP user experience using SAP Build Work Zone. Authentication and authorization are handled centrally through SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Prerequisites and Readiness Checklist

Before proceeding with the setup, ensure the following prerequisites are in place:

Authorizations

  • SAP BTP Global Account Administrator
  • SAP BTP Subaccount Administrator
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS & IPS) Administrator
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Administrator

Entitlements

  • SAP Joule (Digital Assistant Service – das-application)
  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Landscape Requirements

  • Supported for both 2-System Landscape (2SL) and 3-System Landscape (3SL)
  • SAP Cloud Connector is not required for Public Cloud systems

Technical Preconditions

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant registered in BTP System Landscape
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant available and accessible
  • Browser allows iFrames and third-party cookies

Preparation

SAP BTP Subaccount Strategy

A dedicated SAP BTP subaccount is typically used to host Joule and SAP Build Work Zone. From a design perspective, regional alignment with SAP Cloud Identity Services is recommended to minimize authentication latency and reduce cross-region dependencies.

The subaccount acts as the orchestration layer where:

  • Joule services are provisioned
  • Work Zone acts as the user entry point
  • Trust and identity integration converge

Identity Trust and Domain Alignment
Single Sign-On between SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP BTP, and SAP Build Work Zone is achieved through trust establishment with SAP Cloud Identity Services.

A critical design consideration here is domain alignment:

  • The IAS domain selected must align with the domain model of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant
  • Mismatched domains are a frequent root cause of repeated authentication prompts and intermittent Joule access issues

Trusted domain configuration on both BTP and IAS is essential for enabling secure cross-origin communication and embedded user experiences.

Identity Attribute Readiness
Joule and SAP Build Work Zone rely on a consistent identity model provided by IAS. Certain core user attributes must be available and consumable by downstream applications.

From an operational perspective:

  • Attribute completeness matters more than attribute volume
  • Missing verification-related attributes can result in silent authentication failures that are difficult to trace at runtime

Ensuring identity consistency early on significantly reduces troubleshooting effort later.

Joule Enablement Model
Guided Onboarding via Booster

SAP provides a Joule Booster that automates the majority of the technical enablement steps. This guided onboarding mechanism establishes the baseline configuration required for Joule to operate within the BTP subaccount.

Conceptually, the booster:

  • Validates entitlements and platform readiness
  • Establishes system formations
  • Creates the necessary connectivity artifacts
  • Activates SAP-delivered communication scenarios

 

Architecturally, this step should be treated as a managed onboarding process rather than a manual configuration exercise.

SAP Build Work Zone Integration

SAP Build Work Zone serves as the experience layer through which Joule is surfaced to end users. Key design elements include:

  • Subscription to the Work Zone service
  • Secure consumption of S/4HANA Cloud content
  • Centralized role-based navigation and access control.

Work Zone consumes S/4HANA Cloud content using SAP-delivered exposure mechanisms, ensuring that only authorized applications, catalogs, and groups are visible.

Content Exposure Model
Content exposure from SAP S/4HANA Cloud into SAP Build Work Zone is governed by standard communication scenarios delivered by SAP. These scenarios enable controlled sharing of Fiori content without custom integration.

From a best-practice perspective:

  • Content exposure should be validated, not modified
  • Role and catalog assignments remain the authoritative control mechanism

This approach preserves clean-core principles while enabling rich user experiences.

Identity Provisioning Architecture
Identity Provisioning Services (IPS) are used to synchronize users and role assignments into SAP Build Work Zone.

Rather than being treated as a one-time activity, identity provisioning should be viewed as:

  • A continuous synchronization mechanism
  • A dependency for consistent authorization behavior
  • A key operational component for Joule visibility

Operational validation of IPS jobs and logs is a critical checkpoint during the initial enablement.

Validation and Operational Readiness
Once the platform setup is complete, validation should focus on the end-to-end user experience rather than on individual configuration items.

Typical validation checkpoints include:

  • Successful single sign-on from SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Visibility of SAP Build Work Zone content
  • Availability of the Joule interaction entry point
  • Execution of basic conversational queries

A successful validation confirms that Joule is correctly embedded and operational.

 

Common Operational Observations
Based on real-world implementations, the following patterns are frequently observed:

  • Repeated login prompts usually indicate trust or domain misalignment
  • Missing Joule entry points are commonly linked to role assignment or identity synchronization gaps
  • Empty or incomplete content typically points to content exposure or IPS issues

Systematically addressing these areas resolves the majority of post-enablement issues.

entry.title: Extending SAP Joule with SharePoint Grounding and MCP
entry.content:
What You Will Learn

In this blog post you will learn how to:

  • Ground SAP Joule in SharePoint documents using SAP AI Core Document Grounding so that Joule can answer questions directly from your document library
  • Build a chunking and embedding pipeline in SAP AI Launchpad that keeps the vector index fresh as documents change
  • Create custom Joule skills and agents in SAP Build that chain document search, structured extraction, and S/4HANA API calls into a fully automated workflow — no copy-paste, no form-filling
  • Connect a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server as a Joule toolkit so that warehouse operations can evolve independently of the agent definition

By the end of this two-part series, a warehouse operator can type "Process delivery note 54321" in Joule and have an inbound delivery created in S/4HANA automatically.

Prerequisites

Before you start, make sure you have:

  • An SAP BTP subaccount with entitlements for SAP AI Core (Extended plan) and SAP Build Process Automation
  • An SAP AI Launchpad instance connected to your AI Core service
  • An SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant with the Inbound Delivery A2X API enabled, or access to SAP API Business Hub sandbox
  • A Microsoft 365 tenant with a SharePoint site containing your delivery note documents (PDF, DOCX, XLSX, or PPTX)
  • Global Administrator or Privileged Role Administrator access in Microsoft Entra ID — or a contact who has it — to grant admin consent for API permissions
  • Basic familiarity with SAP Build (Joule Studio) and BTP destinations

Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP's generative AI copilot embedded across the SAP ecosystem. It understands SAP transactional data well out of the box — but enterprise knowledge often lives outside SAP. Delivery notes, vendor contracts, material specs, and SOPs typically sit in SharePoint, Teams, or network drives. Joule cannot act on what it cannot read.

This walkthrough shows how we connected those two worlds. The result is a Joule Capability Package called Custom Agent Ideation that imports three reusable building-block packages and adds two orchestration agents and four Joule skills on top:

  • searchDeliveryNotes — instant vector search over SharePoint PDFs directly from Joule
  • deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery — end-to-end automation: search → extract → map → create inbound delivery in S/4HANA
  • createInboundDelivery — direct inbound delivery creation when you already have the data
  • warehouseTaskCreator — MCP-powered agent that creates warehouse tasks from an inbound delivery number

Architecture Overview

Joule Extensibility — Reference Architecture

The SAP reference architecture for Integrating and Extending Joule defines the canonical pattern that underpins everything in this walkthrough. At its core, Joule sits in the middle — connected to SAP BTP for extensibility, SAP Cloud Identity Services for authentication, and the broader SAP application estate (S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Concur) through a unified integration layer.

extend joule with joule studio

Source: SAP Architecture Center — Integrating and Extending Joule (Ref-Arch 464deb)

Joule's extensibility operates across four capability patterns:

Pattern Description

TransactionalNatural language triggers direct backend processes — purchase orders, approvals, document creation
NavigationalGuides users to the right application or screen without manual navigation
InformationalDelivers knowledge from SAP Help, customer documents, or indexed content via RAG
AnalyticalSurfaces business insights and performance metrics on demand

This walkthrough implements the Informational pattern (SharePoint document grounding) and the Transactional pattern (automated inbound delivery creation and warehouse task management) — showing how both can work together in a single Joule Capability Package built with Joule Studio in SAP Build.

This Demo — Solution Architecture

The diagram below shows how the components in this walkthrough connect at runtime. The Joule Capability Package (Custom Agent Ideation) sits on top of three reusable building-block packages and exposes four skills that Joule routes to based on the user's intent.

Imported Package Version Purpose

SharePoint Document Grounding Search1.1.0AI Core vector search over SharePoint
Delivery Note Assistant1.1.0SBPA action to retrieve and extract delivery note details
Inbound Delivery (A2X)1.1.3SBPA action to create inbound deliveries in S/4HANA

These three packages are SAP-maintained building blocks. You write only the orchestration logic that is unique to your scenario.

Custom Agent Ideation project overview showing imported package dependencies

Part 1: SharePoint Integration

Step 1 — Set Up SharePoint Grounding

This step produces the four credentials that AI Core needs to pull documents from SharePoint. You will set up a SharePoint site, register an Azure app, grant the right permissions, and validate access before wiring anything into AI Core.

1a — Prepare the SharePoint Site

  1. Sign in to sharepoint.com and create a new Communication site (recommended for document repositories).
  2. Upload your delivery note files. Supported formats include PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, and images.
  3. Note the site URL: https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site-name> — you will need it in Step 2.

1b — Register an Azure App in Microsoft Entra ID

  1. Go to Azure Portal → Entra ID → App registrations → New registration.
  2. Name the app (e.g., SAP-DocumentGrounding) and complete the registration.
  3. From the Overview page, copy the Application (client) ID and Directory (tenant) ID.
  4. Go to Certificates & Secrets → Client Secrets → New client secret.
    • Copy the Value immediately — it is shown only once. The Secret ID (GUID) is not the secret value.
  5. Go to API Permissions → Add a permission → Microsoft Graph → Application permissions.
    • Add Sites.Selected (Application type — "Access selected site collections").
    • Do not use Sites.Read.All — Sites.Selected is the least-privilege option and the correct permission for Document Grounding.
    • Click Grant admin consent. The Sites.Selected permission must show a ✅ status before you continue.
Caution: Admin consent requires a Global Administrator or Privileged Role Administrator. If you do not have that role, you cannot approve this yourself.
  • SAP employees: raise a request with your SharePoint / Azure Entra ID admin team to grant consent on the app registration.
  • External partners / customers: work with your organisation's SharePoint administrator to click Grant admin consent on your behalf.
Without this step, all API calls will return 403 Forbidden even if the permissions appear in the list.

Azure App Registration overview page showing Application and Directory (tenant) IDsAzure API permissions showing Sites.Selected granted with admin consent

1c — Grant the App Access to the SharePoint Site

Admin consent in Entra ID grants API-level access, but you also need to grant site-level read permission using PnP PowerShell:

Install-Module PnP.PowerShell

Connect-PnPOnline -Url "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site-name>" -Interactive

Grant-PnPAzureADAppSitePermission `
  -AppId "<CLIENT_ID>" `
  -DisplayName "SAP Document Grounding" `
  -Site "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site-name>" `
  -Permissions Read

1d — Validate Access (Optional but Recommended)

Get an access token and confirm the Graph API can reach your site before wiring it into AI Core:

# Get access token
curl -X POST "https://login.microsoftonline.com/<TENANT_ID>/oauth2/v2.0/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=<CLIENT_ID>&client_secret=<CLIENT_SECRET>&scope=https://graph.microsoft.com/.default"

# Validate site access
curl -X GET "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/sites/<SHAREPOINT_DOMAIN>:/sites/<SITE_NAME>" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <ACCESS_TOKEN>"

A 200 response with site metadata confirms everything is wired correctly.

At the end of this step you should have four values ready:

Value Where to find it

Tenant IDEntra ID → App registration → Overview
Client IDEntra ID → App registration → Overview
Client SecretEntra ID → Certificates & Secrets → Value column
SharePoint Site URLSharePoint Admin Center or browser address bar

These four values feed directly into the AI Core secret in the next step.

Part 2: AI Core Service Integration

Step 2 — Create the SharePoint Grounding Generic Secret in AI Launchpad

AI Core needs your SharePoint credentials to pull documents during indexing. Store them as a Generic Secret in SAP AI Launchpad so they are never hardcoded in pipeline configuration.

  1. Open SAP AI Launchpad → AI Core Administration → Generic Secrets.
  2. Click Create and name the secret, e.g., sharepoint-grounding-secret.
  3. Paste in the credentials JSON:
{
  "tenantId": "<AZURE_TENANT_ID>",
  "clientId": "<CLIENT_ID>",
  "clientSecret": "<CLIENT_SECRET>",
  "sharepointSiteUrl": "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site>"
}

AI Launchpad Generic Secret form showing Application (Client) ID fieldAI Launchpad Generic Secret form showing Client Secret field

  1. Save. This secret will be referenced by the chunking pipeline in Step 3.

Step 3 — Create the Document Chunking Pipeline

The chunking pipeline reads documents from SharePoint, splits them into semantically meaningful chunks, embeds them, and writes them to a vector collection in AI Core.

  1. In AI Launchpad, navigate to Pipelines → Create Pipeline.
  2. Select the Document Grounding template.
  3. Set the source to SharePoint and reference the secret from Step 2.
  4. Set chunk size and overlap. Recommended starting values: 512 tokens, 50-token overlap.
  5. Name the target collection — e.g., delivery-notes.
  6. Run the pipeline and wait for completion. Note the final document and chunk counts.
Tip: If you update documents in SharePoint regularly, schedule the pipeline to run on a recurring cadence so the vector index stays current.

Step 4 — Verify the AI Core Search Endpoint

Once the collection is populated, verify the search endpoint is working. This is what the searchDeliveryNotes skill will call at runtime.

  1. In AI Launchpad, go to Grounding → Collections and select delivery-notes.
  2. Note the Collection ID — you will use this as the id in the grounding filter in Part 2, Step 5.
  3. Set the retrieval config: Top K = 5, Similarity threshold = 0.7.
  4. Run a test query — e.g., "delivery note vendor material PO number" — and confirm chunks return correctly.

The SharePoint Document Grounding Search package imported in the project wraps this endpoint as a callable Joule function.

 

Part 3: SAP Build

Step 5 — Build the Actions

Actions in SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) wrap external API calls as reusable steps. The project imports two action packages.

5a — SharePoint Delivery Note Search (from Delivery Note Assistant package)

This action calls the AI Core Orchestration endpoint and returns the delivery note content as structured JSON. Under the hood it combines document grounding with an LLM extraction step in a single request.

  • Action identifier: post_getDeliveryNoteDetails
  • Destination: mocked_JOULEDELIVERYNOTEDEST (points to your AI Core endpoint)
  • Input: query (string) — the search phrase passed by the agent
  • Output: deliverycontentdetails — the extracted delivery note fields as a JSON string

The SBPA function is called asynchronously via the Joule skill integration gateway and uses long-polling (14 s timeout) to wait for the AI Core response.

The full request body sent to AI Core:

{
  "orchestration_config": {
    "module_configurations": {
      "llm_module_config": {
        "model_name": "gpt-4o",
        "model_params": { "max_tokens": 1000, "temperature": 0 }
      },
      "grounding_module_config": {
        "type": "document_grounding_service",
        "config": {
          "input_params": ["query"],
          "output_param": "groundingOutput",
          "filters": [
            { "id": "<YOUR_COLLECTION_ID>", "data_repository_type": "vector" }
          ]
        }
      },
      "templating_module_config": {
        "template": [
          {
            "role": "system",
            "content": "You are a delivery note extraction assistant. Extract all fields from the document context and return ONLY a valid JSON object with no markdown, no extra text, no code fences."
          },
          {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Document context:\n{{?groundingOutput}}\n\nExtract all delivery note details and return as JSON."
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "input_params": { "query": "<natural language search query>" }
}

Key points to note:

  • filters.id — replace <YOUR_COLLECTION_ID> with the Collection ID from Step 4.
  • data_repository_type: "vector" — must be snake_case. Using dataRepositoryType (camelCase) returns a 400 error.
  • {{?groundingOutput}} — the AI Core Orchestration service injects retrieved document chunks here before calling the LLM.
  • The system prompt forces a clean JSON response with no markdown fences, because the downstream agent parses the content string directly.

5b — Create Inbound Delivery (from Inbound Delivery (A2X) package)

This action calls the S/4HANA A2X Inbound Delivery API to create the delivery record.

  • Action identifier: post_A_InbDeliveryHeader
  • Destination: mocked_inbound_delivery (points to your S/4HANA or API Hub endpoint)

Field Source in agent S/4HANA field

supplierdeliverydocumentDeliveryNoteNoDeliveryDocumentBySupplier
deliverydateDeliveryDate (ISO format)DeliveryDate
actualdeliveryquantityDeliveryQtyActualDeliveryQuantity
purchaseorderPONumberReferenceSDDocument
purchaseorderitemLineNoReferenceSDDocumentItem
batchnumberBatchNoBatch

Step 6 — Create the AI Agent Project in SAP Build

The project in SAP Build groups everything together. Create a new AI Agent Project and import the three dependency packages.

  1. In SAP Build, go to Lobby → Create → Build an Automated Process → AI Agent.
  2. Name it Custom Agent Ideation, set description to Customized Joule Agent for Public Cloud ERP.
  3. Import the three dependency packages:
    • SharePoint Document Grounding Search
    • Delivery Note Assistant
    • Inbound Delivery (A2X)
  4. Configure the system aliases under Settings:
    • SbpaService → destination: SAP_SBPA_API
    • APIAGENTCALLBACK → destination: AC_callback_api

These aliases let the function configuration in SAP Build refer to the SBPA integration gateway without hardcoding URLs.

Step 7 — Build the Delivery Note to Inbound Delivery Agent

This is the orchestration brain of the solution. The agent runs the full Search → Extract → Map → Create → Confirm flow automatically, without any user intervention.

Agent configuration:

agent: Delivery Note to Inbound Delivery Agent
base_model: gpt-5-minimal
iterations: 25
default_output_format: markdown

The automated process the agent follows:

  1. On any delivery note query, immediately call Search Delivery Notes with the query: "delivery note number, vendor, material, quantity, PO number, line item"
  2. Parse the JSON response and extract: DeliveryNoteNo, DeliveryDate, PONumber, LineNo, MaterialNo, DeliveryQty, BatchNo, Warehouse
  3. Map the extracted values to the inbound delivery API fields (see table in Step 5b)
  4. Call Create Inbound Delivery with the mapped parameters
  5. Reply to the user with a confirmation including the delivery note number, PO, quantity, and date

The agent instruction explicitly states: "The agent must never ask the user for any of these values or present options; the process must be strictly automated and sequential."

Content safety filtering is applied to all inputs (Azure Content Safety, threshold 4 for hate/self-harm/sexual/violence).

Expertise:

You are an expert in automating logistics workflows, specializing in the seamless extraction, mapping, and orchestration of delivery note data to inbound delivery processes without user intervention.

Instructions:

**Purpose & Objectives**
The Delivery Note to Inbound Delivery Agent assists users in fully automating the creation of inbound deliveries from delivery notes. Its objectives are:
- Automatically search for and extract delivery note information upon receiving relevant queries.
- Parse, map, and transfer all required delivery fields for inbound delivery creation, without requesting further input from the user.
- Ensure successful creation is promptly confirmed to the user.

**Process**
1. When the user requests a delivery note-related task (such as providing a delivery note number, vendor, material, PO, or line item):
   - Immediately call the 'Search Delivery Notes' tool with the query: "delivery note number, vendor, material, quantity, PO number, line item".
2. Upon receiving the JSON output from the tool:
   - Parse the JSON string and extract these fields:
     - DeliveryNoteNo
     - DeliveryDate
     - PONumber
     - LineNo
     - MaterialNo
     - DeliveryQty
     - BatchNo
     - Warehouse
   - Convert *DeliveryDate* with same format of '2026-04-27T00:00:00'
   - Map the extracted values as follows for the next step:
     - SupplierDeliveryDocument = DeliveryNoteNo
     - DeliveryDate = DeliveryDate
     - ActualDeliveryQuantity = DeliveryQty
     - PurchaseOrder = PONumber
     - PurchaseOrderItem = LineNo
     - BatchNumber = BatchNo
3. Using the mapped values, automatically invoke the 'Create Inbound Delivery' tool with the mapped input parameters.
4. Upon successful execution, confirm to the user what was created (including delivery note and PO details).
- The agent must never ask the user for any of these values or present options; the process must be strictly automated and sequential as above.

**Tooling**
- 'Search Delivery Notes': Retrieves delivery note and related document data based on query parameters.
- 'Create Inbound Delivery': Consumes extracted and mapped values to generate inbound deliveries.
- JSON Parsing: The agent must be capable of handling and extracting the required fields from complex JSON responses and mapping them precisely as outlined.
- The orchestration logic must guarantee a hands-free, predictable workflow for the user.

Additional Context:

**Communication**
- Maintain a professional, concise, and transactional tone; avoid any unnecessary small talk or ambiguity.
- Always state exactly what was done and which identifiers were involved.
- Never prompt the user for information or present choices about delivery note or inbound delivery details.

**Guidelines**
- If any required value is missing in the JSON output, clearly state which information was unavailable, but do not request user input to supply it.
- Follow the sequence of tools and logic strictly: Search → Extract → Map → Create → Confirm.
- Avoid speculation or assumptions—work strictly with the output you have.

**Examples**
**User:** "Please process this delivery note."
**Agent:** *[Searches delivery notes, extracts all required fields, creates inbound delivery, then replies:]*
"Inbound delivery created for Delivery Note #54321 (PO #98765, Qty: 100, Date: 2024-06-10)."

**User:** "Can you make an inbound delivery from this note?"
**Agent:** *[Performs end-to-end flow automatically; responds with confirmation of creation and details.]*

**Edge Case:**
**User:** "Process this delivery note."
**Agent:** *[If deliveryNoteNo is missing after parsing JSON:]*
"Unable to create inbound delivery: delivery note number was not found in the search results."

Step 8 — Create the Joule Skills (Scenarios)

Skills are what Joule sees — each scenario maps a user intent to a function, which in turn calls an agent or directly invokes an action. The project exposes four skills:

Skill (Scenario) Joule trigger description Target function

searchDeliveryNotesUser asks about delivery notes, vendors, materials, or shipmentssearchDeliveryNotes (direct action)
deliveryNotetoInboundDeliveryUser wants to convert a delivery note to an inbound deliverydeliveryNotetoInboundDelivery (agent-orchestrated)
createInboundDeliveryUser wants to create an inbound delivery directlycreateInboundDelivery (direct action)
warehouseTaskCreatorUser wants to create a warehouse taskwarehouseTaskCreator (agent-orchestrated via MCP)

Each agent-orchestrated skill works the same way at the function level: take the raw user message, pass it to the agent, and return the agent's response as markdown.

# deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery function (simplified)
- type: agent-request
  name: deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery
  body: <? agentRequestBody ?>
  result_variable: agentResponse

Step 9 — Build the Warehouse Task Creator Agent (MCP)

The Warehouse Task Creator is a second agent that handles a different part of the workflow: once an inbound delivery exists, create the corresponding warehouse task via the MCP server.

Agent configuration:

agent: Warehouse Task Creator
base_model: gpt-5-minimal

Process:

  1. Receive a user request containing an inbound delivery number
  2. Validate that the number is present and correctly formatted (numeric)
  3. Call the MCP warehouse task creation tool
  4. Return the task ID, or a clear error message if something goes wrong

MCP toolkit binding:

toolkits:
  - name: WarehouseTaskCreator
    type: mcp
    config:
      description: Create warehouse task based on inbound delivery document number.
      destination: warehouse-task-mcp
      namespace: warehouseTaskCreator.WarehouseTaskCreator0
      path: /mcp
      timeout: 60

The type: mcp is what distinguishes this agent from the previous one. Instead of calling an SBPA action group, it calls a Model Context Protocol endpoint at /mcp on the warehouse-task-mcp destination. Joule discovers available tools dynamically via the protocol — new warehouse operations can be added to the MCP server without touching the agent definition.

Expertise:

You are an expert in creating warehouse tasks in the MCP system, specializing in processing inbound delivery numbers and efficiently triggering warehouse operations based on user requests.

Instructions:

**Purpose & Objectives**
The Warehouse Task Creator assists users in automatically creating new warehouse tasks in the MCP system using the inbound delivery number provided in their prompt.
Success criteria include:
- Promptly creating a warehouse task whenever a valid inbound delivery number is given.
- Ensuring the user receives clear confirmation about task creation, including task details or error messages.

**Process**
1. Receive the user's request containing an inbound delivery number.
2. Validate that the inbound delivery number is present and correctly formatted.
3. When the number is valid, call the MCP warehouse task creation tool and submit the inbound delivery number as input.
4. Relay the response from the MCP tool back to the user, summarizing task details or clearly stating any encountered issues.
5. If input is missing or invalid, ask the user for clarification or a valid inbound delivery number.

**Tooling**
- MCP Warehouse Task Creation Tool: Use this tool to create a warehouse task by inputting the provided inbound delivery number. Always match the user's request to the required format before calling the tool.

Additional Context:

**Communication**
- Keep responses concise, straightforward, and informative.
- Avoid ambiguity—if the inbound delivery number is missing or incorrect, state the issue directly.

**Guidelines**
- Never guess or invent inbound delivery numbers; always ask the user to provide one if missing or unclear.
- Confirm task creation clearly and include key details or relevant error messages.

**Examples**
**User:** "Create a warehouse task for inbound delivery 5273849"
**Agent:** "A new warehouse task has been created for inbound delivery 5273849. Task ID: WT-00012345."
**User:** "Create a task for inbound delivery 123xyz"
**Agent:** "The inbound delivery number '123xyz' is invalid. Please provide a valid numeric inbound delivery number."

Step 10 — Configure the MCP Destination

The MCP server exposes warehouse operations as MCP-compliant tool definitions. The BTP destination is what connects the SAP Build agent to your deployed server.

  1. Deploy the MCP server to BTP (Cloud Foundry or Kyma). The tool namespace is warehouseTaskCreator.WarehouseTaskCreator0.
  2. In BTP Cockpit, create a destination named warehouse-task-mcp pointing to your deployed MCP service URL.
  3. Configure authentication (Bearer token recommended).
  4. In SAP Build, the warehouseTaskCreator agent picks up this destination automatically via the type: mcp toolkit binding.
  5. Test end-to-end by triggering the warehouseTaskCreator skill in Joule with a real inbound delivery number.

End-to-End Walkthrough

With everything deployed, here is what a warehouse operator experiences in Joule:

Scenario A — Search for a delivery note:

"Show me delivery note from Vendor ABC for PO 4500012345"

Joule routes to searchDeliveryNotes → SBPA calls AI Core Document Grounding Search → returns chunks from the SharePoint PDF → Joule displays the delivery note details.

Scenario B — Fully automated inbound delivery creation:

"Process delivery note 54321"

Joule routes to deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery → agent searches SharePoint → extracts and maps all fields automatically → calls S/4HANA A2X API → confirms:

"Inbound delivery created for Delivery Note #54321 (PO #98765, Qty: 100, Date: 2026-04-27)."

Scenario C — Warehouse task creation:

"Create a warehouse task for inbound delivery 5273849"

Joule routes to warehouseTaskCreator → agent validates the number → calls MCP server → confirms:

"A new warehouse task has been created for inbound delivery 5273849. Task ID: WT-00012345."

Summary

Step Component What Was Built

1Azure / SharePointApp Registration with Sites.Selected for least-privilege grounding access
2AI LaunchpadGeneric Secret storing SharePoint credentials
3AI Core PipelineDocument chunking and embedding pipeline → delivery-notes vector collection
4AI Core SearchVector search endpoint verified and ready
5SAP Build Actionspost_getDeliveryNoteDetails (SharePoint) + post_A_InbDeliveryHeader (S/4HANA A2X)
6SAP Build ProjectCustom Agent Ideation with three imported packages
7Joule AgentdeliveryNotetoInboundDelivery — automated Search → Extract → Map → Create
8Joule SkillsFour scenarios: search, end-to-end automation, direct create, warehouse task
9Warehouse AgentwarehouseTaskCreator — MCP-powered agent for warehouse operations
10MCPwarehouse-task-mcp destination + deployed MCP server

Key Design Decisions

Why two separate agents instead of one?

The delivery note flow is deterministic and fully automated — it always follows Search → Create with no branching. The warehouse task flow is simpler and uses a completely different backend (MCP vs. SBPA). Keeping them separate means each agent has a tight, auditable task definition and an independent iteration budget.

Why MCP for warehouse tasks?

MCP lets the warehouse operations backend evolve independently. New tools — cycle counting, pick confirmation, stock queries — can be added to the MCP server without touching the Joule agent definition. Joule discovers new tools dynamically via the protocol.

Why import packages instead of building from scratch?

The three imported packages (SharePoint Document Grounding Search, Delivery Note Assistant, Inbound Delivery (A2X)) are SAP-maintained building blocks. Using them gives you tested action definitions and upgrade paths for free. You write only the orchestration logic that is unique to your scenario.

Next Steps

  • Extend the document scope — add more SharePoint libraries (purchase orders, goods receipts) by adding source configurations to the chunking pipeline.
  • Chain the scenarios end-to-end — route deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery output directly into warehouseTaskCreator so that a single Joule prompt covers the entire inbound process.
  • Add locale formatting — the project already includes a localize function that formats dates and numbers according to the user's regional settings before displaying results.

Related Links

  • SAP Architecture Center — Integrating and Extending Joule
  • SAP AI Core Documentation — Document Grounding
  • SAP Build Process Automation — Joule Studio
  • SAP Joule Extensibility Overview
  • Model Context Protocol (MCP) — Official Specification
  • Microsoft Graph API — Sites.Selected Permission
  • PnP PowerShell — Grant-PnPAzureADAppSitePermission

Have questions or feedback? Drop a comment below — happy to help. You can also find me and my teammates (liling.jia@sap.com, xiaoming.chai@sap.com) on the SAP Community profile page.

entry.title: Multi-System Joule Support for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's enterprise landscape, organizations rarely operate with just one SAP S/4HANA system. Whether driven by complex organizational structures, regional requirements, or company acquisitions, businesses typically maintain multiple S/4HANA Cloud systems across their landscape. Each system serves specific purposes: perhaps one handles finance for EMEA, another manages operations in North America, and yet another supports a recently acquired subsidiary.

Until recently, Joule faced a significant limitation: it could only connect to a single SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud system. This meant that users working across multiple systems couldn't leverage Joule's AI capabilities uniformly across their entire landscape. Organizations found themselves in a challenging position: either create multiple Joule instances (one for each S/4HANA system) or accept limited coverage of their system landscape.
The good news? Joule now supports multi-system connectivity for both SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (SAP Help Documentation). With this enhancement, you can configure one Joule instance to connect to multiple S/4HANA systems, providing your users with a unified, intelligent assistant experience across their entire landscape.
 

What Multi-System Support Enables

With multi-system Joule, you can:
Unified Experience: One Joule instance serves all your S/4HANA Private Cloud systems
Intelligent System Selection: Joule automatically determines the right system based on user prompts and authorizations
Cross-Landscape Coverage: Users access scenarios across different systems in a single conversation
Simplified Administration: Manage one Joule instance instead of multiple isolated instances

This blog post will guide you through the step-by-step process of activating multi-system support for Private
Cloud Edition, complete with best practices to optimize your setup and ensure your users get the most out of
this powerful capability.
 

Architecture Overview

The architecture shows how a single Joule instance connects to multiple SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud systems through SAP Build Work Zone and BTP destinations, enabling unified access across your entire system landscape.

How Multi-System Joule Works

Multi-system Joule fundamentally changes how the digital assistant interacts with your system landscape:

System Awareness
Joule capabilities become system-aware, meaning they're visible and available based on specific conditions, primarily user authorizations and system access. When a user opens Joule, they see scenarios relevant to the systems they're authorized to execute.

Intelligent System Selection
When you submit a prompt to Joule, the AI analyzes several factors to determine the appropriate target system:
  1. Your user authorizations across different systems
  2. System descriptions and characteristics configured in the Joule Admin Center
  3. Keywords and context within your prompt
  4. Previously selected systems within the current conversation
Single-System Execution
While Joule can connect to multiple systems, each individual scenario executes in exactly one system at a time. This ensures data consistency and clear transactional boundaries. However, you can run multiple scenarios across different systems within the same conversation.

Disambiguation Flow
If Joule can't confidently determine the target system from your prompt, it will ask you to select the appropriate system. Your selection is remembered for the conversation context, reducing the need for repeated clarifications.
 

Prerequisites

Before you begin setting up multi-system support, ensure you have the following in place:
  • Existing Joule Setup: Joule must already be configured and operational following the Joule Onboarding Guide
  • First S/4HANA System / Client: At least one SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud system already integrated with Joule via the standard integration process
  • Other S/4HANA Systems / Clients: Prepare the integration of other systems / clients by following the integration steps outlined in the Integration Guide for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Please Skip number 8 (Run the Joule Booster) and the creation of the SSO Destination (part of step 9) as these steps will be covered during this blog.
  • Admin Roles: Joule Tenant Admin roles assigned to your user for accessing the Joule Admin Center
  • BTP Access: Administrator access to your SAP BTP Cockpit for destination management
  • SAP Build Work Zone Access: Access to SAP Build Work Zone (standard or advanced edition) for managing content providers and channels

Step-by-Step Activation Process

Step 1: Configure BTP Destinations

The foundation of multi-system Joule is proper BTP destination configuration. Each S/4HANA system requires its own set of destinations, and since BTP doesn't allow duplicate destination names, you must use a clear naming convention.

Understanding the Naming Convention

Pattern: {DESTINATION_BASE_NAME}_{SYSTEM_ID}{SYSTEM_CLIENT}
Examples:
Original default: S4HANA_PCE_SSO
For system XXX123: S4HANA_PCE_SSO_XXX123
For system YYY789: S4HANA_PCE_SSO_YYY789

This naming pattern ensures destinations are:
Unique: Prevents naming conflicts in BTP
Identifiable: Administrators can immediately see which system a destination serves
Consistent: Follows a predictable pattern across all systems
 
! Make sure {SYSTEM_ID}does not include any special characters (./,;:\#) or white spaces !

Rename Your Existing Default Destination

  1. Navigate to your SAP BTP Cockpit → Your Subaccount → Connectivity → Destinations
  2. Locate the default destination created during your initial Joule setup: S4HANA_PCE_SSO
  3. Click the destination name to open its configuration
  4. Click Edit
  5. Update the Name field to include your system ID as a suffix
  6. Example: If your system is XXX with client 123, rename to S4HANA_PCE_SSO_XXX123
  7. Leave all other configuration settings unchanged
  8. Click Save
Screenshot of the new Destination Name

Create Destinations for Additional Systems

For each additional S/4HANA Private Cloud system you want to connect:
  1. Follow the standard destination configuration process for S/4HANA Private Cloud integration
  2. Apply the naming convention with the new system's ID
  3. Example: S4HANA_PCE_SSO_YYY789
  4. Configure all required destination properties (URL, authentication, etc.) to point to the new system
PropertyDescription/Value
NameS4HANA_PCE_SSO_{SYSTEM_ID}{SYSTEM_CLIENT}
TypeHTTP
DescriptionEnter a description.
URLThe URL of the Virtual Host of your Cloud Connector: 
https://<host>:<port>
Proxy TypeOnPremise
AuthenticationPrincipal Propagation
sap-client (additional property)
The client number of the ABAP system.
 
Critical Warning: Do NOT re-run the Joule Booster for additional systems. The Booster should only be used for initial Joule setup. For multi-system configuration, manually create destinations following the documented process.
 

Step 2: Include Systems in Joule Formation

Once your BTP destinations are configured, you need to register the additional S/4HANA systems in the Joule Formation.

Add Systems to Formation

  1. Navigate to your SAP BTP Cockpit → Your Global Account Overview → System Landscape: Formations
  2. Locate your Joule Formation
  3. In the Formation view, click the Include Systems button
  4. The "Include Systems in Formation" wizard opens

Configure Integration Details

On the Integration Details screen:
  1. From SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition [SYSTEM_ID] to Joule
  2. Select the new system you want to add
  3. Enable "Enable Capability Deployment"
  4. This checkbox ensures that Joule scenarios for this system are properly deployed and available
  5. Enable "Enable Joule in the Integrated System"
  6. This checkbox activates the Joule plugin in the SAP Fiori Launchpad for this system
  7. Click Review → Finish

Repeat for Additional Systems

If you have more than one additional S/4HANA system to connect, repeat the above steps for each system.

Joule Formation on BTP for Multi-System Configuration

Step 3: Map Custom System Names

This step establishes the critical link between how SAP Build Work Zone identifies your systems (CDM Content Provider ID) and how Joule refers to them (System Name). This mapping enables Joule to route scenario executions to the correct backend system.

Understanding the Mapping Concept

CDM Content Provider ID: The identifier used in SAP Build Work Zone to represent your S/4HANA system content
System Name: The identifier Joule uses internally to route requests to the correct system

In some cases, these identifiers may differ. The Custom System Name mapping ensures they're properly aligned so Joule can execute scenarios in the correct system.

Get CDM Provider Details from SAP Build Work Zone

  1. Navigate to your SAP BTP Cockpit → Your Subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions
  2. Click on your SAP Build Work Zone subscription to open the application
  3. In the left navigation panel, click Channel Manager
  4. The "Content Channels" view displays all registered content providers
  5. Locate each of your S/4HANA Private Cloud systems in the list
  6. Note the ID value for each system. You'll use these in the next step
Example: You might see entries like:
  • System XXX123 with ID and client: XXX123
  • System YYY789 with ID and client: YYY789
  • Or potentially technical IDs like: sap.start.6c746d76-2

Overview of the Channel Manager Configuration in SAP Build WorkZone

Update Custom System Name in Joule Admin Center

  1. Access Joule Admin Center
  2. Navigate to your Joule Admin Center URL (typically provided during Joule setup or available through the Joule Admin Center documentation)
  3. Select the sap_digital_assistant from the List of Assistants and navigate to the Detail View of your assistant
  4. Click Edit in the top-right corner
  5. Select System Name from the configuration options
  6. Select System Type
  7. In the "Select System Type" dialog, choose sap.s4pce
  8. This filters the view to show only your S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition systems
  9. Enter Custom System Names
  10. For each system listed, enter the Content Channel ID you noted from SAP Build Work Zone
  11. The mapping format is: [Default System Name] → [Custom System Name] (= Content Channel ID)
    Example:
    Default System Name: XXX123→ Custom System Name: XXX123
    Default System Name: YYY789 → Custom System Name: YYY789
  12. Click Save to persist your system name mappings
  13. Click again Edit in the top-right corner
  14. Select System Details
  15. Add a System Title and a meaningful System Description that provides context about the usage of the system
  16. Click Save to persist your system system descriptions

Overview of the System Configuration in the Joule Admin CenterEnsure Default and Custom Destination Mapping

Make sure that the names of your Default Destinations fit to the Destination names you configured in Step 1:

  1. Click Edit in the top-right corner
  2. Select System Name from the configuration options
  3. Verify if the Default Destination name fits to the Destination name you configured in Step 1 (Configure BTP Destinations):
    {DESTINATION_BASE_NAME}_{SYSTEM_ID_A}{SYSTEM_CLIENT_A}
    {DESTINATION_BASE_NAME}_{SYSTEM_ID_B}{SYSTEM_CLIENT_B}
  4. If the Default Destination differs from your configured name, use the Custom Destination column to map your system to the correct Destination name
Overview of the Destination Mapping in the Joule Admin Center

 Important: Fill in values for ALL system entries. Do not leave any entries empty, as this will cause routing failures.


Upon successful save, you'll see the updated mappings displayed in the Joule Admin Center UI, confirming that Joule can now correctly route requests to your S/4HANA systems.

Best Practices for Multi-System Success

The configuration steps above will get multi-system Joule up and running, but following these best practices will significantly improve the user experience and system selection accuracy.

System Naming and Descriptions

The system descriptions you configure in the Joule Admin Center directly influence how well Joule can determine the correct target system. These descriptions are analyzed as part of the AI's decision-making process.

Poor System Description

Finance System

Good System Description

Finance System for EMEA, handling accounts payable and receivable
for sub-brand AutoParts Europe with headquarters in Frankfurt, Germany.
Primary company code: 1010. Used by finance teams in Germany, France,
and United Kingdom.

Why it matters: The detailed description provides context clues that Joule can match against user prompts. Keywords like "EMEA," "Frankfurt," "Germany," "France," and "company code 1010" help Joule route requests accurately.

What to Include in System Descriptions

  • Geographic scope: Regions, countries, or cities the system serves
  • Organizational scope: Subsidiaries, brands, or business units
  • Functional scope: Primary business processes (finance, procurement, sales, etc.)
  • Key identifiers: Company codes, plants, sales organizations
  • User groups: Which teams or departments primarily use this system

User Prompt Best Practices

Educate your end users on how to write effective prompts for multi-system environments.

Vague Prompt

"Show my expenses"
Joule may need to ask which system you're referring to, adding extra steps to the interaction.

Specific Prompt

"Show my expenses for plant 1200 in Frankfurt"
The prompt includes contextual information (plant, location) that Joule can match against system descriptions for automatic routing.

Additional Tips for Users

  • Include location references when relevant ("in Germany," "for the US region")
  • Mention organizational units if applicable ("for the AutoParts division")
  • Reference system identifiers if known ("in QM7002," "company code 1010")
  • Build on conversation context: If you've already specified a system in the conversation, subsequent prompts can be less specific

Role Assignment Strategy

When assigning roles in SAP Build Work Zone, strive for distinct role profiles per system when possible. This helps Joule make more confident system selection decisions based on a user's authorizations.

Example Strategy:
System XXX123 (EMEA Finance): Assign role SAP_BR_AP_ACCOUNTANT
System YYY789 (NA Operations): Assign role SAP_BR_PURCHASING_AGENT

If a user has entirely distinct roles across systems, Joule can more easily determine intent. However, this isn't always feasible, as many users need the same functional roles across multiple systems. In such cases, the system descriptions and user prompt quality become even more important.
 

How System Disambiguation Works

Understanding Joule's system selection logic can help you optimize your configuration and train your users effectively.

Decision Factors

When you submit a prompt, Joule analyzes multiple factors simultaneously:
  1. User Authorizations: Which systems can you access? Which roles do you have in each system?
  2. System Descriptions: Keywords and phrases in the system descriptions configured in Joule Admin Center
  3. Prompt Content: Keywords, locations, identifiers, and context in your natural language prompt
  4. Conversation History: If you've already selected a system in this conversation, Joule considers that context

The Disambiguation Flow

User submits prompt
    ↓
Joule analyzes all decision factors
    ↓
Confidence level HIGH → Execute scenario in determined system
    ↓
Confidence level LOW → Prompt user to select system
    ↓
User makes selection → Selection remembered for conversation
    ↓
Subsequent prompts in conversation benefit from context

When Joule Asks You to Choose

If Joule cannot confidently determine the target system, it will present you with a system selection prompt. This typically happens when:
  • Your prompt is ambiguous and could apply to multiple systems
  • You have identical roles in multiple systems
  • System descriptions don't provide distinguishing characteristics
  • You're starting a new conversation without system context

Example Conversation

Having multiple SAP S/4HANA private cloud systems connected, an example conversation could look like the following, where the business user has equal role assignments in both connected systems. If the initial prompt does not provide an indicator for a preferred system, Joule will prompt the user to select the system:

Example Conversation of the Mullti-System Disambiguation

Limitations

  • With this release, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition now supports multi-system connectivity. However, the connection of multiple tenants from other SAP solutions e.g. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP Ariba, SAP Concur, SAP SuccessFactors etc. are currently not supported.
  • SAP Build WorkZone has a limit of 1000 assigned roles to a user. With connecting multiple system, customers need to ensure that this number won't be exceeded.

Conclusion

Multi-system support transforms Joule from a single-system assistant into a true enterprise-wide digital companion. By connecting multiple SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud systems to a single Joule instance, you provide your users with seamless access to AI-powered assistance across their entire system landscape.

Additional Ressources

SAP Help Documentation

entry.title: Boost Case Resolution with Business Information Extraction in SAP Service Cloud V2
entry.content:
What you will get from this blog

In this blog, you’ll grasp how Business Information Extraction (BIE), powered by generative AI in SAP Service Cloud Version 2, turns unstructured service inputs via emails into structured, case-ready data.  Btw, for sales use case - quotation automation, please check this blog here How Business Information Extraction in SAP Sales Cloud V2 Accelerates Sales Response and Conversion

Therefore, focusing on the Service Scenarios – Case Classification, this use case delivers and addresses the following: 

  • Automatically creating and enriching cases
  • Classifying tickets to Category Level 1 (with deeper levels planned for future enhancement) and Case Type automation
  • Reducing manual triage work
  • Improving routing quality and time-to-resolution

By the end, you’ll have a clear understanding of how to:

  • Use BIE to interpret customer issues from free text via emails
  • Auto-classify tickets to the right Level-1 category
  • Configure scenarios in SAP Service Cloud V2
  • Connect this to measurable business benefits and KPIs for your service operation

Process Story: from messy service email to structured case

Service requests almost never arrive as “perfect” structured records.

They look more like this:

"Hi, our water pump in Plant 17 keeps shutting down with error E-2043. This started after last week’s maintenance. We already tried restarting and cleaning the filter but it still fails every few hours. Please log this against our existing maintenance contract and mark as urgent – this is impacting production"

From an agent’s perspective, there’s a lot hidden inside that paragraph:

  • What’s the product or asset?
  • Is there a serial ID or reference?
  • Is this linked to a contract or account?
  • How severe or urgent is this?
  • Which Category Level 1 (for example: “Hardware Issue”, “Software Issue”, “Maintenance Request”, “Billing & Contract”, “How-to Question”) should this be routed to? 

Traditionally, someone needs to:

1. Read the entire email carefully
2. Interpret the problem, context, and urgency
3. Manually create a case
4. Fill in header fields, choose the right L1 category, possibly link contract/account/product
5. Only then can the “actual work” of resolution begin

This is where Business Information Extraction (BIE) in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 becomes really relevant.

With BIE active for service, the flow looks more like this:

1. Customer sends an email 
2. Generative AI analyses the subject and description
3. BIE extracts key elements (for example, Product, Serial ID, Contract Account External ID)
4. BIE derives a Category Level 1 classification from the same text
5. A case is created and pre-filled – ready for the agent to review and act 

The agent hasn’t disappeared from the process. What changes is that:

- They start from a “good first draft” case
- Classification and key header data are already filled
- Their time goes into resolving, not decoding!

Business Benefits in Services Processes

The value of BIE in service is not just extracting fields – it’s how it reshapes the entire intake and triage process.

Once extraction and are embedded early, we typically see positive impact in:

In other words, BIE doesn’t just extract data; it stabilizes, accelerates, and de‑frictions the service intake flow from first contact to initial handling.

Use case: Case Categorization

For this blog, we’ll focus specifically on one core use case:

Automatically categorizing incoming cases from free-text descriptions.

SAP Service Cloud V2 allows you to define and use standard elements and classes such elements as Product Name, Product ID, and Serial ID and classes like Case Type and Category Level 1 for cases. Powered by generative AI, where BIE reads the case subject/description and maps it to the appropriate elements and classes.

Today, the generative AI-based BIE scenario in cases supports:

Standard element examples

- Product Name
- Product ID
- Serial ID
- Requested Date

Standard class examples

- Category Level 1
- Case Type

On top of that, you can introduce custom elements and classes based on your own header-level extension fields (string fields), such as:

Custom element examples: Installed Base Reference, Location Code, Customer Priority Code, SLA related information.

Custom class examples: Request Channel Type Email, commercial and service-related context like contract-linked, project-driven, warranty-assigned, and ad-hoc, customer info and Internal Support information.

Elements, classes, and scenarios – why it matters

BIE in SAP Service Cloud V2 is designed around three concepts that you can shape to your own service reality:

1. Elements – explicit values to extract
- Example (standard): Product ID, Product Name, Serial ID
- Example (custom): Location, internal reference number, external ticket ID

2. Classes – values derived from meaning and context
- Example (standard): Category Level 1, Case Type
- Example (custom): Customer Tier, Support Entitlement, Priority Category

3. Scenarios – how these are combined and used
- You define a scenario for a specific usage context (Case, Business Document, or Joule)
- You decide which elements and classes belong to that scenario

For service, this means a simple scenario might just include elements: Product Name, Product ID, Class: Category Level 1
 and a richer scenario can add elements: Serial ID, Contract Account External ID, custom string fields and Classes: Case Type, custom case classification fields relevant to your process.

This flexibility lets you align BIE with very different service setups, for example:

  • High-volume, simple support (e.g., SaaS or telco)
  • Heavy emphasis on Level‑1 category + queue routing
  • Asset- or field-service–heavy environments
  • Strong focus on product, serial ID, location, contract, and urgency
  • Contract- and entitlement-driven operations
  • Classification towards contract-related queues, escalations, or specific SLAs

Same capability, different scenarios – configured directly by your SAP Service Cloud V2 administrators / AI expert.

Value accelerator path: activating BIE for service cases in Sales Cloud V2

Understanding the use case is one side; getting it live is what creates value.
Below is a simplified activation and configuration path for Business Information Extraction in cases:

Note: You can’t keep a generative AI–based BIE scenario with first-level case category active together with the classical machine learning–based case categorization for the same use case. Therefore is important to assess and decide which approach you want to use.

Measuring outcomes: KPIs to track from day one

Activating the use case is important – proving its value is what drives adoption. Therefore, focus on a small, clear KPI set at the beginning. For a service scenario like this, four KPIs are particularly useful:

1. Time from request receipt to case creation & routing - Is the intake and initial assignment faster after BIE activation?

2. Manual effort per case at intake - How many minutes do agents spend on creating and classifying a case now vs. before?

3. Rework due to wrong category or routing - How often are cases reassigned or re-categorized because the initial Category Level 1 was wrong, and are those numbers going down over time?

4. First-pass case completeness (tailored to your process) - Define what a “complete enough to work on” case looks like (e.g., filled Category L1, product, contract reference) and track how often BIE-created cases already meet this bar on first pass

Hence, start with one bounded scenario (for example, a particular region or product line), define your baseline, measure the delta after BIE activation, and refine from there. This phased approach keeps things practical and avoids turning the initiative into a long, theoretical design project.

Final thoughts

Business Information Extraction for service is about closing a very real gap:

- The gap between how customers describe their issues (unstructured text)
- And how service systems need that data to drive routing, SLA handling, and resolution (structured categories and fields)

By automatically extracting key elements and assigning the Case Type and Category Level 1 from incoming text, BIE in SAP Service Cloud V2:

- Reduces friction at the very first touchpoint
- Gives agents a better starting point with richer, pre‑filled cases
- Improves routing, reduces triage overhead, and shortens time to first meaningful response

Most importantly, because BIE is configurable, you are not limited to a one-size-fits-all categorization model. You can shape elements, classes, and scenarios around your service organization – starting simple with Level‑1 classification and case header fields, and extending deeper as your needs and the product evolve.

For teams running SAP Service Cloud Version 2, this makes BIE for ticket classification a pragmatic, high‑impact AI use case to activate: practical, configurable, and measurable from the first day of go‑live.

Thanks for reading!

entry.title: From Champions to Scale – How to Operationalize Joule for Consultants Across Project Teams
entry.content:
Reading time: 5–7 minutes

Having a handful of enthusiastic Joule for Consultants users is a good start, but it is not the same as adoption. In this blog, we share practical metrics, common adoption patterns, and proven scaling practices for SAP Practice Managers, Team Leaders, and Chief AI Officers looking to maximise ROI from their J4C investment.

 

The Real Challenge Starts After the Initial Pilot

Across the market, expectations around AI have become more pragmatic. Customers and partners no longer look for experimentation—they expect tangible value during implementation delivery. Joule for Consultants (J4C) is the practical AI assistant from SAP that already supports faster delivery and improved productivity in project execution.

In our previous blog, Joule for Consultants Adoption in Partner SAP Practices, we described J4C adoption as a leadership journey moving from cautious pilots to sustained, high-usage delivery. 

SAP partners who already started with J4C already will know that the real challenge is not creating a few champions or early enthusiasts. The difficult part is scaling their success into a consistent way of working across project teams. That step does not happen organically—it requires measurement, deliberate action, and structural change. 

We will look at four key areas:

  • How to measure adoption using the right metrics
  • How to turn those metrics into actionable insights
  • How new J4C capabilities support scaling
  • What a practical scaling path looks like

 

Making Real Usage Visible: Adoption Metrics

Scaling cannot be managed without data. To understand adoption, SAP provides J4C consumption data via SAP for Me, including user counts, generated messages, and AI unit consumption. 

However, raw data alone is not enough. Evaluating key metrics in context enables meaningful interpretation and better discussions with partners. Here is how we look at the adoption data in the context of an SAP practice:

Metric

Definition

SAP Practice Size

Number of SAP practitioners in the partner's SAP practice, as stated in the partner business plan

J4C Provisioned Users

Users assigned as eligible to consume AI Units through J4C — this represents the entitled user base

J4C Active Users

The subset of provisioned users who generated at least one message (prompt) in a given month — the metric that reflects actual usage

Provisioned Users (% of Practice)

Share of provisioned users relative to total practice size; enables benchmarking across partners of different sizes and measures how broadly J4C is embedded in the organization

Ratio Active Users vs Provisioned Users

Reveals the utilization of J4C subscriptions. If this ratio is low, the company is paying for unused access.

Messages per Month

Total number of interactions submitted by all users in a given month

Messages per Active User

Average number of messages per active user; indicates engagement intensity — are users working with J4C regularly or only occasionally?

Projects with J4C

How many implementation projects are actively supported by J4C, and in which areas?

Active Users per Project

How intensively J4C is used within a specific project — a critical signal because real value comes not from ad hoc use by enthusiasts, but from deliberate usage embedded in delivery

Additional project-team metrics, such as task or prompt completion rates, reduction in support / helpdesk requests or perceived productivity gains, can be captured manually to help validate expected productivity improvements.

Scaling is not about how many champions exist—it is about how consistently J4C is used across SAP teams and customer projects. These metrics provide a clear view of adoption maturity and readiness to generate ROI. 

 

What the Data Tells You: From Insight to Practice

Metrics alone are not enough—they only become valuable when translated into insights and action. There are a few typical patterns, which we see quite consistently, so let’s discuss what they mean.

Pattern 1: Large gap between provisioned users and active users

When active users are far below the provisioned base, the issue is structural: the partner is consuming AI Units but not realizing value from much of the investment. Scaling has happened commercially, but not operationally. Inactive users reduce ROI.

Tactical actions: Gather feedback from provisioned users, enable them on role- and project-specific use cases, and reassign licenses from chronically inactive users to colleagues more likely to benefit.

Best practice — Feedback loops: Use a simple recurring survey and ask provisioned users to submit   weekly feedback. Users who do not answer can lose their license allocation. And if there is negative feedback, sharing it with SAP helps inform the product backlog.

Best practice — License rotation: Some partners replace users who do not engage meaningfully, keeping momentum and consumption active across the provisioned pool.

Pattern 2: Active user count declining, but messages per active user increasing

This pattern suggests a committed core group is emerging - fewer people are experimenting, but those who remain have found valuable use cases and rely on J4C regularly.

Tactical actions: Learn from these users and share their prompt libraries, workflows, and success stories through team meetings, internal channels, or a shared prompt library.

Best practice — J4C included into the delivery toolchain: Practices that scaled J4C embedded it into formal delivery — proposals, project kick-offs, and the standard project toolset. Making it official and announced to customers, accelerates adoption beyond individual enthusiasm.

Pattern 3: Downward trend in both active users and messages

A decline in both metrics usually means licenses were assigned to the wrong users or teams lack sufficient visibility and enablement.

Tactical actions: Reevaluate and reassign licenses with the project view in mind — the goal is consistent usage in delivery, not broad distribution as a benefit. SAP partner manager can support this with anonymized benchmarking across similar partners.

Best practice — run a formal pilot: Do not leave adoption to chance. Set up a formal pilot with a defined leader and team, then systematically explore use cases and manage adoption.

Pattern 4: No data visible to practice leadership

If no one tracks adoption data, the likely issue is missing ownership: the business case was approved, but accountability for usage did not continue after purchase.

Tactical action: Appoint a J4C “champion” to review adoption data regularly and escalate issues. Your partner manager can provide guidance on accessing consumption data in SAP for Me.

Pattern 5: Solid active user base, but usage concentrated on a few projects

When individual adoption is healthy, but project coverage is narrow, motivated consultants are using J4C on their own initiative, but it is not yet part of project design or staffing.

Best practice — Embed J4C in project design: Successful partners budget J4C support at the proposal stage instead of leaving adoption to individual choice during execution. The focus shifts from “who wants a license?” to “which projects require J4C support?”—a key step toward operational maturity.

Use your data to identify the pattern that best fits your situation, then make a proactive management decision rather than waiting passively.

A note for partners yet to start a pilot: If your practice has not yet initiated a J4C pilot, SAP offers co-funding via Development Funds to help eligible partners offset their initial investment: Explore Joule for Consultants Initiative 2026

 

What's New in J4C: Features Supporting Scaling

J4C continues to evolve, with new capabilities shaped by partner feedback and real-world usage. 

At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced new J4C features and outlined the roadmap through early 2027. You can watch the session recording here, or see the continuously updated list in the Joule section on the SAP Help Portal.

Recent enhancements fall into two categories: individual productivity improvements and features designed to enable team-wide scaling.

User experience improvements: J4C now supports up to 50 active conversations, making it easier to manage several client, project, or topic-specific threads in parallel. A new Retry button allows users to retry, refine, shorten, or expand responses and edit original prompts without restarting the conversation. These improvements may appear incremental, but friction is the most common reason active users lapse.

Expert Workspace: Now generally available, Expert Workspace allows creation of personalized AI configurations tailored to a specific client, workstream, or domain — with custom instructions, reference files, and persistent context across conversations.

Peer-to-Peer Expert Sharing: Consultants can share Experts with colleagues, giving each teammate an independent copy to adapt to their own project context. This helps move effective configurations from individual power users to wider teams without administrative overhead.

Centralized Expert Library (Administrators): Administrators can curate and publish Expert configurations for all users, making senior consultants’ best workflows available practice wide. This supports standardization and helps new or less frequent users start from tested examples.

Custom Knowledge Grounding: Administrators can connect J4C to internal repositories such as Microsoft SharePoint, SAP Build Work Zone, or AWS S3 via SAP AI Core document grounding. This enables answers that combine company-specific assets, reusable project material, and SAP’s curated knowledge.

The H2 2026 roadmap also includes SAP Landscape Awareness, with a pilot starting in June 2026. This capability is intended to help J4C understand the customer’s system landscape and provide more personalized, context-aware and project-specific guidance for consultants.

Together, these capabilities help scale J4C beyond individual champions by making effective practices easier to reuse, share, govern, and embed into everyday project delivery.

 

Closing: From Productivity to Operational Excellence

The shift from individual champions to consistent project-level usage determines whether J4C becomes a competitive advantage or remains an isolated productivity tool. It requires structured management, clear accountability, and integration into delivery processes. 

Key actions to take:

  • Track the right metrics
    Monitor provisioned users, active users, engagement levels, and project coverage regularly
  • Diagnose before acting
    Identify your current adoption pattern and tailor actions accordingly
  • Scale what works
    Use Expert Sharing and libraries to institutionalise successful workflows
  • Embed J4C into project design
    Plan usage at proposal stage and align with delivery structures
  • Stay engaged with SAP
    Leverage feedback channels, pilot programmes, and benchmarking to accelerate adoption

Ultimately, organisations that capture the full value of Joule for Consultants will be those that convert individual productivity gains into a repeatable delivery model. 

 

Final note: Content of this article has been supported by Joule for Consultants. Wording and sentences have been refined using AI.

Here are examples of J4C prompts used to write this blog:

  • Which new J4C features have been made available in 2026?
  • How does J4C support team collaboration and on an implementation project?
  • Suggest how an SAP S/4HANA migration project manager should measure J4C adoption within the project team. Purpose is to evaluate and improve productivity gains in an implementation project. Use SAP best practices and propose simple, readily available, high-impact metrics.
  • Consultants of an SAP partner are using J4C with their own internal subscriptions. Where can this partner see data about their J4C / AI Unit usage? Not at customers, but his own consumption. Be specific and include navigation instructions.

entry.title: SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026 - Wave 1
entry.content:
Hi everyone, on behalf of the CX Product Success team at SAP, I'm delighted to share with you our latest deep dive videos as part of the SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026. 

Wave  #1 (April-May 2026)

1. Gainsight Customer Success Management for SAP Sales Cloud Version 2

 

Join Emma Falldorf, Product Manager SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 at SAP, for an exciting overview of the latest integrations between Gainsight Customer Success Management and SAP Sales Cloud Version 2. In this insightful video, we dive deep into the key benefits and usage scenarios of this powerful integration, provide a comprehensive look at how Gainsight enhances the customer experience.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:39 - Why did SAP invest in bringing Gainsight into SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?
02:06 - Which concrete scenarios does Gainsight offer for SAP customers?
03:09 - How can customers adopt Gainsight in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?

06:54 - Demo: Customer Success Management in Gainsight
10:27 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

2. Account Planning in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 in Action

Join Crystal Zhang, Product Manager SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 at SAP, for an exciting overview of the latest Account Planning capabilities of SAP Sales Cloud Version 2.
In this SAP Product Inspiration Video, learn why intelligent account planning is essential for modern sales teams and how SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 brings structure, insight, and focus to key account management. Through a concise end-to-end demo, the video shows how sales teams can capture customer goals and insights, define strategies and sales plans, monitor pipeline health, collaborate through action plans, and generate AI-powered executive summaries—all within one integrated solution. Discover how Intelligent CX from SAP helps turn account insights into consistent, measurable sales success.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:56 - Why Account Planning in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?

09:00 - Demo: Account Plan - Generate AI-based Executive Summary
09:43 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

3. AI Scouting in SAP Sports One

Join Michael Kesselmann, Product Manager SAP Sports One, and Achim Ittner, VP for Sports at SAP, for an exciting overview on embedded AI scouting capabilities in SAP Sports One. In this video, you will learn how clubs and federations can leverage Generative AI to streamline scouting workflows, accelerate decision-making, and create high‑quality player summaries with ease.
Discover how SAP Joule enhances the scouting process by consolidating extensive written reports, answering performance-related questions instantly, and generating structured management summaries that support faster and more informed squad planning decisions.

The key highlights shared in this video are:

05:05 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

4. Mondi's Business Transformative with SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2, SAP Commerce Cloud and SAP Joule

Join Philipp Haspel (Strategic Advisor and CX Presales Expert at SAP) in an inspiring discussion with Richard Drosdek (Head of Customer Experience at Mondi) about the CX Transformation at Mondi. Watch this customer-led product inspiration video to learn why Mondi chose SAP Customer Experience solutions and how SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, SAP Commerce Cloud, and the SAP CX AI Toolkit are helping them modernize sales, service, and customer engagement.

The key highlights shared in this video are:

entry.title: Explore SAP's New Joule Early Adopter Care Programs: Answers to Your Most Asked Questions
entry.content:
If you followed the announcements at Sapphire 2026, you've probably noticed that Joule is evolving rapidly. Between Joule Work, Joule Studio, and the new desktop experience, many customers are asking the same question:

"Where do I start, and which of these programs is relevant for my organization?"

To help answer that question, SAP has launched several Early Adopter Care (EAC) programs that give selected customers early access to upcoming Joule innovations while working directly with SAP product teams.

In this guide, I'll break down the four currently available Joule EAC programs, explain the differences between them, clarify common areas of confusion, and help you determine which opportunity best aligns with your business and AI adoption goals:

  1. Program Overview & What's New
  2. Registration & Process
  3. Eligibility & Prerequisites
  4. Joule Work & Joule Studio
  5. Joule with Knowledge Graph
  6. SAP Business AI Agents
  7. Joule Work Desktop App
  8. Trust, Data & Security
  9. Support & Next Steps
  10. Continue Your Joule Learning Journey

 

1 — Program Overview & What's New

Q1 I already have Joule — what's actually new, and why should I join an EAC?

Classic Joule (the version most customers have today) is a scenario-based assistant: you trigger predefined Joule skills within individual SAP applications — for example, asking Joule a question inside S/4HANA or SuccessFactors. It works well within an app context but requires users to know where to go and what Joule can do there.

The new generation announced at Sapphire 2026 is a fundamental shift in three areas:

  • Joule Work (Engagement Layer): A single, full screen entry point that sits above all your SAP applications. Instead of going to an app and then asking Joule, you express your intent directly and Joule surfaces the right data, agents, and actions from across connected systems. It introduces Spaces: dynamic workspaces that aggregate cross-application context for a specific goal. See how Joule takes action across systems to achieve people’s goals.
